content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 607 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 14, 1977

Gary M. Gallery
Legal Counsel
California Community Colleges
1238 S Street
Sacramento 95814

Dear Mr. Gallery:

This is in response to your letter of April 12, 1977,
regarding the obligation of a board member whose term has
expired to file a financial disclosure statement.

At its meeting on June 8, 1977, the Commission con-
sidered this question and concluded that the board member
is required to file an initial disclosure statement and a
leaving office statement.

The board member may combine into one statement the
required two filings by disclosing his investments and
interests in real property as of March 28 (the effective
date of the Board of Governors' conflict of interest code)
and his investments and interests in real property and
income for the period March 28 through April 8 (the date
his successor was appointed and he officially left office).

Please notify the board member that his disclosure
statement should be filed within 30 days of the date of
this letter.

Thank you for bringing this matter to the Commission's
attention. If you have any questions, please feel free
to contact me.

Sincerely,

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE ADMINISTRATION 916 322 5660 • [illegible] [illegible] [illegible] • [illegible]

CHANCELLOR'S OFFICE

CALIFORNIA COMMUNITY COLLEGES
1238 S STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

April 12, 1977

Mr. H. Michael Bennett
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Bennett:

We seek your concurrence in our conclusion relative to filing requirements
pursuant to Section 87204 of the Government Code (see also Section 50507
of Part VI, Title 5, of the California Administrative Code) on whether a mem-
ber of the Board, whose membership has been terminated by virtue of gubernatorial appointment of his successor, need file, at all, a disclosure statement.

Essentially, a Board member's term has ended prior to the initial disclosure
statement filing deadline, and the question is, since he is not (on no duty
to do so) filed any disclosure statement, must he file a final statement within
the meaning of Section 87204? The Board of Governors adopted a Conflict of
Interest Code in June of 1976, which was approved by your Commission on January 19, 1977, and filed by the Secretary of State on February 25, 1977, with
an effective date of March 28, 1977 (30 days after filing by the Secretary of
State).

Disclosure statements must be filed within 30 days after the effective date
of the Conflict of Interest Code (see Government Code Section 87302(b); see
also Section 50507(b) of Part VI, Title 5 of the California Administrative
Code).

Filing deadlines for members of the Board when is April 27, 1977.

A Board member, whose term would have expired January 15, 1977, continued to
serve until his successor was appointed (see Section 1302 of the Government
Code). On April 8, 1977, word was received by this office that the Governor
had appointed a replacement for the particular Board member. We understand
the successor has taken the oath of office, which, in my judgment, constitutes
"qualified for office" within Government Code Section 1302. The Board member's
last participation in any meeting of the Board was at the regularly scheduled
March 25 meeting, at which time neither the Conflict of Interest Code was effective, nor any disclosure filing requirements yet operative.

It is our conclusion that no final disclosure statement would be necessary for
such a member for the following reasons: (a) the thrust of Proposition 9 is
to preclude making or participating in any decisions. Here, no decisions will

Mr. Michael Bennett
April 12, 1977
Page 2


be participated in by the Board member at a time following the effective date
of the Agency's own Code, or concurrent with disclosure filing requirements;
(b) Section 37204 requires the statement (for person leaving office) to cover
that period of time during the period since the previous statement was filed.
Here, the time for requirement of the initial (and only statement for this
year) has not yet occurred for members of the Board generally. (c) The initial statement requires only investments and interests in real property, and not
income. The final statement requires all three, a somewhat, as applied to these
facts, incongruous situation.

With your concurrence, we'll so advise the member. Since action is required
within 30 days of leaving office, should there be a finding of required filing,
we would appreciate your early consideration of the matter.

Sincerely,

Gary M. Gallery
Legal Counsel

GMG:lr

May 18, 1977


Gary M. Gallery
Legal Counsel
California Community Colleges
1238 S Street
Sacramento, Calif. 95814

Dear Mr. Gallery:

     This is to confirm our telephone conversation
of today regarding the disclosure filing obligations
of a board member whose term of office may have ended
prior to the effective date of the Community Colleges'
conflict of interest code.

     I indicated that the Commission's legal staff
felt that there was some question as to when the board
member's term of office actually ended. If it ended
after the date the conflict of interest code went into
effect, he may be required to file a disclosure state-
ment.

     The matter will be taken up at the next Commission meeting, scheduled for June 6, 7 and 8, and I will
notify you of the exact date and time as soon as the
agenda has been prepared.

     In the event the Commission determines that
the board member was in office at the time the conflict
of interest code went into effect and is therefore required to file a disclosure statement, the due date will
be 30 days from the date he receives notice of required
filing from the Commission.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Administrative Assistant

CHANCELLOR'S OFFICE

CALIFORNIA COMMUNITY COLLEGES
1238 S STREET
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

April 12, 1977

Mr. H. Michael Bennett
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Bennett:

We seek your concurrence in our conclusion relative to filing requirements
pursuant to Section 87204 of the Government Code (see also Section 50507
of Part VI, Title 5, of the California Administrative Code) on whether a mem-
ber of the Board, whose membership has been terminated by virtue of guber-
natorial appointment of his successor, need file, at all, a disclosure statement.

Essentially, a Board member's term has ended prior to the initial disclosure
statement filing deadline, and the question is, since he has not (on no duty
to do so) filed any disclosure statement, must he file a final statement within
the meaning of Section 87204? The Board of Governors adopted a Conflict of
Interest Code in June of 1976, which was approved by your Commission on Jan-
uary 19, 1977, and filed by the Secretary of State on February 25, 1977, with
an effective date of March 28, 1977 (30 days after filing by the Secretary of
State).

Disclosure statements must be filed within 30 days after the effective date
of the Conflict of Interest Code (see Government Code Section 87302(b); see
also Section 50507(b) of Part VI, Title 5 of the California Administrative
Code).

Filing deadlines for members of the Board then is April 27, 1977.

A Board member, whose term would have expired January 15, 1977, continued to
serve until his successor was appointed (see Section 1302 of the Government
Code). On April 8, 1977, word was received by this office that the Governor
had appointed a replacement for the particular Board member. We understand
the successor has taken the oath of office, which, in my judgment, constitutes
"qualified for office" within Government Code Section 1302. The Board member's
last participation in any meeting of the Board was at the regularly scheduled
March 25 meeting, at which time neither the Conflict of Interest Code was ef-
fective, nor any disclosure filing requirements yet operative.

It is our conclusion that no final disclosure statement would be necessary for
such a member for the following reasons: (a) the thrust of Proposition 9 is
to preclude making or participating in any decisions. Here, no decisions will

Mr. Michael Bennett
April 12, 1977
Page 2


be participated in by the Board member at a time following the effective date
of the Agency's own Code, or concurrent with disclosure filing requirement;
(b) Section 87204 requires the statement (for persons leaving office) to cover
that period of time during the period since the previous statement was filed.
Here, the time for requirement of the initial (and only statement for this
year) has not yet occurred for members of the Board generally. (c) The initial statement requires only investments and interest in real property, and not
income. The final statement requires all three, a somewhat, as applied to these
facts, incongruous situation.

With your concurrence, we'll so advise the member. Since action is required
within 30 days of leaving office, should there be a finding of required filing,
we would appreciate your early consideration of the matter.

Sincerely,

[Signature]
Gary M. Gallery
Legal Counsel
